focus: Validate bg-index lifecycle and code-graph owner/orphan recommendations
noveltyJustification: Source validation refines the umbrella background-index packet into process-boundary work and tightens orphan/ambiguous-liveness rules for code-graph ownership.
summary: Validated that daemon task ownership, MCP ThreadPool shutdown, and code-graph single-owner/orphan handling need separate acceptance gates despite sharing the lifecycle theme.
Keep the lifecycle theme but split implementation and tests by owner: daemon task registry/shutdown first, MCP child ThreadPool/background runner second.
The existing lease path handles basic single-owner checks, but the packet must prove same effective DB-dir identity and classify orphaned child/server processes without unsafe kills.
Daemon shutdown tracks connection handler tasks but not load-time or explicit index tasks created inside ProjectRegistry.
implication: Shutdown can close registry state after waiting on handlers while index tasks remain unowned or invisible to the shutdown set.

summary: MCP child background indexing runs blocking DaemonClient.index in the default ThreadPool and silently swallows failures.
implication: Async task cancellation is insufficient, and startup index failures or stuck workers are not observable.

summary: The code-graph launcher lease is keyed to the canonical effective DB dir and correctly treats EPERM as live, but it does not by itself prove orphaned child/server ownership after launcher death.
implication: A dead launcher PID can coexist with a live orphaned server, so stale-owner recovery needs child/server identity or health evidence before starting another owner.

summary: system-code-graph shutdown closes the IPC bridge but does not call the existing closeDb() helper.
implication: The DB handle release part of F-013/F-027 remains a required acceptance criterion, separate from launcher lease gating.

summary: Refine packet 3 from one broad implementation into two sequenced gates: daemon-bg-index-task-registry-shutdown and mcp-bg-index-threadpool-shutdown. The first owns task identity and registry/DB invariants; the second owns client-side worker cancellation, bounded await, client close, and visible telemetry.
Classify ESRCH on the recorded owner PID as stale; classify EPERM as live/unknown; classify PPID 1 as orphan_candidate only. Do not kill or reclaim solely from PPID 1 or EPERM.
If a launcher dies after spawning the server, the lease owner PID can be stale while the child server is still alive and holding the same DB.
mitigation: Record child/server identity or owner token, add health/bridge/readiness checks keyed by effective DB dir, and default ambiguous cases to LEASE_HELD_BY or dry-run diagnostics instead of launching a duplicate.

summary: Cancelling an asyncio wrapper around run_in_executor can leave the blocking worker running while shutdown appears successful.
mitigation: Request daemon-side cancel, close the client transport, bound-await the future, and log unresolved worker state; tests must use a blocking client.index fixture to prove behavior.

summary: MCP/daemon background-index tests should cover load-time auto-index shutdown, explicit index shutdown, client disconnect plus shutdown/remove, visible _bg_index exception logging, blocked ThreadPool worker timeout, no duplicate startup _bg_index, and post-cancel/post-remove re-index/search usability.

Code-graph owner tests should cover duplicate launchers for the same canonical DB dir, env override and symlink aliases, EPERM liveness, PPID-1 orphan candidate dry-run behavior, dead owner plus live orphan child classification, SIGKILL child-survival backstop, and shutdown invoking closeDb().

hypothesis: Fix daemon and MCP background indexing as one undifferentiated code change
reason: The daemon owns ProjectRegistry task identity and DB safety, while the MCP child owns a blocking ThreadPool worker and stdio process shutdown; the acceptance tests and failure modes differ.

hypothesis: PPID 1 or EPERM is enough to reap a stale code-graph process
reason: PPID 1 proves orphaning but not inactivity, and EPERM means liveness is permission-denied/ambiguous; both require non-destructive reporting or dry-run classification unless additional same-DB health evidence exists.

hypothesis: Existing launcher lease tests fully prevent duplicate DB owners
reason: They cover live owner, DB-dir override, dead-PID reclaim, cleanup, and strict-disable behavior, but not orphaned child/server survival or same-realpath aliasing across effective DB dirs.
